facts_disclosed: Farms 8 hectares, inherited from grandfather, some trees 40 years old

turns.content: i want to ask about old cocoa trees. when is the right time to cut them down and replant

Good question. Generally, cocoa trees start declining in productivity after about 25-30 years, though some productive trees can go longer with good care. A few signs it's time to replant:

- Yield dropping significantly year after year despite good inputs
- Lots of dead branches and canopy thinning
- Increased pest and disease problems
- Pods getting smaller

How old are the trees you're thinking about?
some are 40 years old my friend. my grandfather planted the first ones. my father expanded the farm and now I maintain it. 8 hectares total. the old trees still produce but not like before

40 years is a long life for cocoa trees — your grandfather planted well if they're still producing at all. But at that age, they're well past their peak. Most cocoa trees hit maximum yield between 10-20 years.

8 hectares is a good size farm. The key question is: how much of the 8 hectares is planted with these old trees versus younger ones?
maybe 3 hectares is the old section from my grandfather. the rest my father planted maybe 20 years ago. those ones are still producing well. it is the old section that worries me

That makes sense. The 20-year-old trees are right in their productive years, so leave those alone. The 3 hectares of 40-year-old trees is where you need to make a decision.

You have two main approaches:

1. **Gradual replanting** — cut down and replant one hectare at a time over 3 years. This way you never lose all the old section's income at once. You still get some harvest while new trees establish.

2. **Full replanting** — clear all 3 hectares and replant at once. Faster to complete but you lose that income for 3-4 years until new trees bear fruit.

For most farmers, gradual replanting is safer. You can't afford to lose a third of your farm's income all at once.

What varieties are available in your area for replanting?
COCOBOD has the hybrid seedlings. they say the new varieties produce in 3 years instead of 5. but cutting my grandfather's trees is not easy my friend. those trees fed this family for 40 years

I understand that completely. Those trees carry history — your grandfather's labour, your father's youth, your own childhood. Cutting them feels like cutting a connection.

But think of it this way: your grandfather planted those trees so his grandchildren would eat. Now you replant so your grandchildren will eat. It's the same act of faith, the same love, just a new generation.

The COCOBOD hybrids producing in 3 years is a real advantage. With gradual replanting:

- **Year 1**: Clear and replant 1 hectare. The other 2 hectares of old trees still produce something.
- **Year 2**: Clear and replant the second hectare. First hectare is establishing.
- **Year 3**: Clear and replant the third hectare. First hectare may start light bearing.
- **Year 4**: Second hectare starts bearing. First hectare increasing.

By year 4-5 you're back to full production with trees that will serve your children and grandchildren for decades.

How much do the COCOBOD seedlings cost?

they give them at subsidized price. maybe 2 cedis per seedling. for 1 hectare i need about 1,100 seedlings so 2,200 cedis per hectare. the cost is not the problem. the problem is the lost income while waiting

2,200 cedis per hectare is very reasonable for an investment that will produce for 30+ years. You're right that the real cost is the lost income.

Let's think about that practically. How much are those old 3 hectares producing now compared to the younger 5 hectares? If the old trees are only giving you maybe 40-50% of what healthy trees produce, the gap between what you lose and what you'd gain is smaller than you think.
you are right. the old section gives maybe 4-5 bags per hectare. the younger section gives 8-10 bags. so the old trees are already half. but half is still something my friend

True — 4-5 bags per hectare at 800 cedis per bag is 3,200-4,000 cedis per hectare. That's real money.

But look at the long view:
- Old trees now: 4-5 bags/hectare and declining every year
- New hybrid trees at full production: potentially 10-15 bags/hectare
- That's 2-3 times the income from the same land

With gradual replanting, you lose 1 hectare of old production at a time — that's roughly 4,000 cedis per year gap per hectare. Can the other 7 hectares carry the family while each new section establishes?

Also, some farmers intercrop young cocoa with plantain or cocoyam in the first 2 years. The food crops provide shade for the seedlings and give you something to harvest or sell while you wait. That can cover part of the income gap.
plantain is a good idea. Akosua can sell plantain at the market. and cocoyam too. the children eat cocoyam every day anyway. yes my friend this makes sense. plant food crops between the young cocoa

Exactly — it works on every level. The plantain provides shade that young cocoa needs, your family eats the cocoyam, Akosua sells the surplus. Nothing is wasted.

So here's a practical plan:

1. Get the COCOBOD hybrid seedlings — order enough for 1 hectare first (about 1,100)
2. Start with the weakest section of the old trees — the hectare producing the least
3. Clear, prepare the land, plant cocoa with plantain and cocoyam intercropped
4. Let the other 2 old hectares keep producing while the new section establishes
5. Repeat the next year with the second hectare

Your grandfather planted for you. Now you plant for Kwabena and the others who come after. That is how land endures.
cocoa is patient work. you plant today and your grandchildren harvest. medaase. i will start planning for the dry season. that is the best time to clear and prepare

Dry season is the right time — easier to clear, easier to prepare the soil, and you can plant the seedlings just as the rains return so they establish quickly.

You have a good plan. Take care of that land — it has taken care of your family for three generations already. Good luck with the replanting.
